title: Rutland Fireplace Mortar Cartridge, 10.3-Ounce, Gray - 63G
description:
About this item
Comes in Gray, Buff and Black
Perfect for repairing small chips or cracked firebrick, re-pointing mortar joints between firebricks, and bonding firebricks together
For interior use only and requires heat to cure
Approximate Coverage: 99 feet with 1/8" bead
Rated to 2000°F (1090°C)
reviews_5s:
The Rutland Fireplace Mortar Cartridge has proven to be an excellent solution for repairing my fireplace. The mortar is easy to apply, adhering well to the existing masonry and creating a strong, durable bond. It fills gaps and cracks effectively, ensuring that the repairs look seamless and hold up to high heat.

One of the things I really like about this product is its consistency. The mortar flows smoothly from the cartridge, making it easy to control and apply precisely where needed. The gray color blends well with most fireplace bricks, providing a professional and clean finish. The only minor downside is that it takes a bit of time to cure fully, so you need to plan accordingly to ensure it sets properly before exposing it to high temperatures. However, the wait is worth it for the reliable and long-lasting results it provides.

I think that the Rutland fireplace mortar is a top-notch product for anyone needing to perform fireplace repairs. Its ease of use, strong adhesion, and ability to withstand high heat make it a dependable choice.Worked great, way to use!Worked amazingly repairing the cracks in the fire board inserts!Easy to use.Works wellThe high heat sealant works great for fire brick in my fire pitI had a crack in the rear reflector panel of our wood burning fireplace. We live in the blue ridge mountains and use our green to heat our living room when ever it’s below freezing outside. We burn only well seasoned oak and hickory so the fire get pretty hot and this is the second time in three years the panel has cracked. Last time we replaced the panel. This time I used this product as directed to patch the crack which at its wides was about a quarter inch in width and ran from the top to the bottom of the panel. I used a flexible spackle knife to cut it in and then waited 24 hrs for the primary cure. Then made a small fire and kept it burning for 12 hrs. I let them fireplace “sit” for another 24 hrs and then did a normal burn. 15 fires later and the patch has held perfect with no spalls or cracks.

Update. After many fires the patch has held well. Follow the curing instructions and this stuff works as advertised.So, I have a French chalet red-brown brick with a gray sand grout..I prepped the loose brick took it out of front mantle, then applied
First thing I noticed was coloring was going to be different.

Gray blueish silver grout caulk.
So I let dry..same..nothing happened to softened the color.

Last resort , reapplied just grout lines And while fresh and wet stick..I took the left over ash and took the fine broom and dusted the grout lines..
Now matches lol 👌

So think ..I your fireplace you use alot will have a dust residue..perfect solutionOthers mention it bubling.. that is 100% due to not letting it self dry for 24 hours or spiking the heat too fast.
I used this to seal cracks in a fireplace that came in a home I just purchased. Some of the thicker areas that were around a quarter inch however, I did not do multiple layers, and it took. Had a fire last night with kindling for 2 hours and then kicked it up with a few bigger sticks for an hour and it hardened up just right.

I did use a plastic bowl and spoon with water to help smooth/push the mortar along the lines I made which was very helpful.

Very pleased- first time doing something like this.The curing process is a little tricky. To control the temperature of your curing fire. It did bubble during curing but otherwise stayed in tact.
reviews_1s: I know this is part of the product description but this stuff turns to chalk if not heated up to fire temps. What this means is that if you are fixing cracks on the outside of your fireplace, this stuff just does not work. It really has a very narrow application, which is inside a fireplace. Why? You might as well get cement that is designed for fireplaces instead, so that you can use it anywhere. The only good thing about this stuff is that it comes in a tube instead of a bucket.It’s pretty much grey silicone. Bubbled up in my fireplace, would not recommend.Product was great, easy to use and the dark gray matched the rock, but months later the color is chalk color and now stands out so I guess I'll be removing it.This is a smooth silicone type of caulk and nothing that looks like mortar as the description on the tube states it is. There is no mortar type of texture or look when it dries and the final color is a very dark gray nothing like color of dry mortar that I have in my fireplace box bricks or surrounding limestone. Have used other cement type of caulk in a tube that has sand and other material that feels like mortar/cement and when dry looks like cement or mortar. Used it to re-point fireplace bricks that had mortar deteriorated between them. Will have to see if this holds up once I use my fireplace after heat curing as required per the instructions.I’ve used this product to seal cracks inside my fireplace for years and in the past it worked, BUT not this time. Followed directions carefully, and product flaked off. I tried again, it failed the 2nd time too. Missed the return window by a few days, contacted manufacturer about the specific lot #, and waiting for a reply. I’ve wasted many hours prepping, removing failed product, then prepping again, and removing it again.Used to seal layers of brick in an outdoor fire-pit. Let it cure for over a week, then burned wood to further cure (according to instructions). A week later, the bricks were visibly loose, and I was able to simply lift them apart with no resistance at all from the sealant. Not sure if this product was not explicitly meant for this purpose, but it did not work at all for the expected use.I liberally used it to repair a chiminea out door fireplace that was cracked . I covered every little crack working it in. . I let it sit and dry for days . First burn and it was all cracked again ."Not for exterior use" sign is only shown in product detail description. It can be missed easily. It did not cure well and was a mess to remove it for my exterior fireplace mortar fix.I used this to install a cleanout on my chimney. It didn't even last 1 week before it started to chip off and crumble from just the sun. The chimney isn't even in use currentlySet up poorly. My experience suggests this stuff is probably only good as crack filler. Item was not described adequately or appropriately. Just get mortar.
